alder-select.name: Alder Select
alder-select.description: Consistent color and grain with minimal natural characteristics. Color ranges from pale yellow-honey to a warm golden-tan — shift toward yellow/honey, NOT strongly reddish. A subtle warm undertone is fine but the dominant impression should be golden, not red. Soft straight grain, even texture. Great for staining.

alder-natural.name: Alder Natural
alder-natural.description: Noticeably more character than Select grade — must be visibly different from Select. Prominent pin knots and sound knots scattered across the face, obvious mineral streaking, and clear color variation ranging from pale yellow to reddish brown. Staves should show contrasting heartwood and sapwood tones. The knots and streaking should be unmistakable, not subtle. Should look distinctly 'natural' — not clean or uniform like Select.

alder-premium-knotty.name: Alder Premium Knotty
alder-premium-knotty.description: Sound knots which may contain star cracks. Some color and grain variation with limited small mineral streaks. Pin knot clusters limited. Premium selection of allowable characteristics.

alder-rustic-knotty.name: Alder Rustic Knotty
alder-rustic-knotty.description: Rustic grade characterized by open dead knots which may contain cracks — these should look rough and natural, NOT clean or polished. Drastic color and grain variation between staves, ranging from pale honey-yellow to warm reddish-brown. Visible mineral streaks throughout. May contain pin knot clusters and large knots with no limit in size. The overall look should be rugged and imperfect — if it looks too clean or uniform, it's wrong. This is the most character-heavy Alder grade.

cherry-select.name: Cherry Select
cherry-select.description: Minimized color variation. Reduced allowance for pin knots. May display small amounts of pitch pockets on the face.

cherry-natural.name: Cherry Natural
cherry-natural.description: Featuring a larger amount of variation in natural characteristics, Cherry Natural may contain pitch pockets, closed knots, pin knots, sapwood shown on the face and more color variation than other grades of Cherry.

maple-select.name: Maple Select
maple-select.description: Least amount of color and grain variation. Color should be consistent light cream with a subtle golden warmth — think pale straw or light honey-cream. CRITICAL: The entire door must be one uniform color — the frame (rails and stiles) must be the SAME light tone as the center panel. Do NOT darken or add tan to the frame. The frame and panel should look like they came from the same board. Great for clear or light stain.

maple-natural.name: Maple Natural
maple-natural.description: Light cream color with subtle golden warmth — pale straw or light honey-cream base. IMPORTANT: The frame (rails and stiles) and center panel must be the same consistent base color — do not make the frame darker or more tan than the panel. Allowance for mineral streaks, worm tracks, and varying amounts of heartwood (darker reddish-brown patches) and sapwood visible on the face. May have small closed knots or pin knots. Should show noticeably more natural character than Maple Select — the mineral streaks and heartwood/sapwood variation should be clearly visible, not subtle.

maple-paint-grade.name: Maple Paint Grade
maple-paint-grade.description: Lowest visual grade of maple — intentionally unattractive. Base tone is light cream with subtle golden warmth (same light maple base as other grades). Over this light base: a few light mineral streaks (2-3 visible streaks, not covered in them), some green or grey discoloration patches, mismatched staves with color contrast between adjacent pieces. May have putty fills, pin knots, and worm tracks on face. This grade should look like it needs to be painted — if it looks clean or pretty, it's wrong.

mdf-panel-maple-paint-grade-frame.name: MDF Panel / Maple Paint Grade Frame
mdf-panel-maple-paint-grade-frame.description: Keep the EXACT same door style, shape, and panel profile from before. Only change the materials: The center panel material is MDF (medium-density fiberboard) — grainless, uniform warm tan-brown color like pressed wood fiber, no wood grain visible on the panel. The frame (rails and stiles) material is Maple Paint Grade — light cream with subtle golden warmth, light grain texture, and one visible mineral streak. The panel and frame should be visually distinct in material but the door geometry, profiles, and proportions must remain identical to the learned style.

mdf-panel-soft-maple-frame.name: MDF Panel / Soft Maple Frame
mdf-panel-soft-maple-frame.description: MDF panel paired with Soft Maple Frame for painting purposes.

red-oak-select.name: Red Oak Select
red-oak-select.description: Wheat-pink to muted pinkish-brown color tones, color-sorted for uniformity — consistent tones across staves without wild swings. The grain pattern VARIES — it may include cathedral arches, straight grain, and mixed patterns across different staves. Do NOT make all grain straight and vertical like Rift Sawn — that is wrong for Select. Open grain pattern with small linear pores, suited to staining. Limited mineral streaking allowed. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots.

red-oak-natural.name: Red Oak Natural
red-oak-natural.description: Red Oak Natural features warm wheat-tan to muted pinkish-brown tones — NOT vibrant pink or saturated red. The color should lean more golden-tan with only a subtle pink warmth, not loud or rosy. Noticeable color variation between staves — some pieces lighter wheat-golden, others slightly warmer pinkish-tan. Must show visible mineral streaking, small closed knots, pin knots, and some sapwood on the face. Should look distinctly more rustic and varied than Red Oak Select — if it looks clean and uniform, it's wrong. The natural character (knots, streaks, color shifts) should be clearly evident, not subtle.

red-oak-quarter-sawn.name: Red Oak Quarter Sawn
red-oak-quarter-sawn.description: MANDATORY: prominent, highly visible medullary ray flake (tiger stripe pattern) — this is the defining feature and MUST be present. Flakes should be bold, frequent, and unmistakable across the face of every stave, not subtle or sparse. Without visible flakes it is NOT Quarter Sawn. Straight grain alone is Rift Sawn; arched grain is Plain Sawn. Wheat-golden to warm pinkish-tan color with a subtle red undertone. Well-defined, darker grain specks for contrast. Select grade characteristics with reduced allowance for color variation, mineral streaks, and knots.

red-oak-rift.name: Red Oak Rift
red-oak-rift.description: Straight vertical grain lines ONLY — this is non-negotiable. NO medullary ray flake (that is Quarter Sawn) and NO arched cathedral grain (that is Plain Sawn). If you see flake or arches, it's wrong. Tightest, most parallel lines of any Red Oak grade with well-defined, darker grain specks for contrast. Select grade characteristics with reduced allowance for color variation, mineral streaks, and knots. Wheat-golden to warm tan color, leaning more yellow than red.

white-oak-select.name: White Oak Select
white-oak-select.description: Warm golden-tan color, like medium honey or light caramel — NOT pale washed-out straw and NOT dark brown. A clear amber warmth should be present. Straight grain pattern with coarse to medium-coarse texture. Small linear pores, ideal for staining. Reduced allowance for small closed knots, pin knots, color and grain variation. Some mineral streaks may be visible.

white-oak-natural.name: White Oak Natural
white-oak-natural.description: Warm golden-tan base color, like medium honey — same amber warmth as White Oak Select but with more variation between staves. Some pieces may be lighter honey-gold, others deeper tan-brown. Coarse to medium-coarse texture with small linear pores, more ideal for staining than painting. Allowance for more color and grain variation, some sapwood on face, small closed knots, and pin knots.

white-oak-rift.name: White Oak Rift
white-oak-rift.description: Color should be warm golden-tan, like medium honey or light caramel with clear amber warmth — NOT pale washed-out straw and NOT dark brown. Straight vertical grain lines ONLY on ALL parts — the frame (rails and stiles) AND the center panel must all show straight, parallel grain. NO arched cathedral grain anywhere on the door, including the frame. NO medullary ray flake (that is Quarter Sawn). If you see arches or flake on any part of the door, it's wrong. Tightest, most parallel lines of any White Oak grade. Minimal allowance for color variation, small closed knots, and pin knots.

white-oak-quarter-sawn.name: White Oak Quarter Sawn
white-oak-quarter-sawn.description: Warm golden-tan color with clear amber warmth, like medium honey or light caramel — NOT pale washed-out straw and NOT dark brown. MANDATORY: prominent, highly visible medullary ray flake (tiger stripe pattern) — this is the defining feature and MUST be present. Flakes should be bold, frequent, and unmistakable across the face of every stave, not subtle or sparse. Without visible flakes it is NOT Quarter Sawn. Straight grain alone is Rift Sawn; arched grain is Plain Sawn. Small allowance for color variation. Reduced allowance for pin knots, small closed knots, and mineral streaks.

european-steamed-beech.name: European Steamed Beech
european-steamed-beech.description: Relatively straight and even appearance, may contain chevron patterned grain. Consistent tan to reddish brown color from steaming process. Similar to Alder with small flecks in grain.

walnut-select.name: Walnut Select
walnut-select.description: Heartwood is LIGHT brown to MEDIUM chocolate brown with occasional purplish casts — do NOT make it too dark. Walnut should feel warm and rich, not near-black or overly dark. Aim for the lighter end of the walnut spectrum. No sapwood on face. Grain pattern with straight and wavy sections. Reduced allowance for small knots and pin knots. Warm, saturated tones.

walnut-natural.name: Walnut Natural
walnut-natural.description: Heartwood is LIGHT brown to MEDIUM chocolate brown with occasional purplish casts — do NOT make it too dark. Walnut should feel warm and rich, not near-black or overly dark. Aim for the lighter end of the walnut spectrum. Creamy white sapwood visible on face, noticeable mix of heartwood and sapwood. Grain pattern with straight, wavy, and additional pattern sections. Allowance for small knots, pin knots, mineral, and other natural characteristics.

soft-maple.name: Soft Maple
soft-maple.description: Distinctly YELLOW-CREAM to HONEY-toned — warmer and noticeably more golden than standard Maple. The yellow/honey tone is the key differentiator from regular Maple. May have light pink and/or gray mixed in. Greater mineral streaking and generally has fleck in the grain.

white-birch-select.name: White Birch Select
white-birch-select.description: Cut from sapwood, consistent creamy white to light tan color with noticeable warm PINKISH-RED undertones — this warmth is the defining characteristic. NOT pure white or cool-toned. The pink/red warmth should be clearly visible, not subtle. Minimal color variation with reduced allowance for pin knots and mineral streaks.

natural-birch.name: Natural Birch
natural-birch.description: Cut from heartwood and sapwood with calico mix of Pink and White tones. May contain small closed knots, pin knots, mineral streaks, and color variation.

knotty-pine.name: Knotty Pine
knotty-pine.description: Yellowish white color. Medium-coarse texture with even grain. Allowance for larger closed knots, knots with star cracks, color variation, pitch pockets, and pin knots.

pecan-hickory-select.name: Pecan-Hickory Select
pecan-hickory-select.description: Frame (rails and stiles) is warm tan-brown with a clear pinkish-red undertone — the pink-red warmth should be obvious but secondary to the tan-brown base. Not purely tan, not purely pink — a warm brown that clearly leans pinkish. Center panel is noticeably lighter: pale creamy-tan to light straw with gentle calico color variation and occasional pinkish-warm streaks. Overall the door is primarily warm tan-brown but with enough pinkish-red warmth that you'd never mistake it for plain oak or alder. The contrast between the darker frame and lighter panel is gentle, not stark. Visible but not heavy grain. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots. Some mineral streaking allowed.

pecan-hickory-natural.name: Pecan-Hickory Natural
pecan-hickory-natural.description: Stark variations between warm tan-brown heartwood with a clear pinkish-red undertone (obvious but secondary to the tan-brown base — not purely tan, not purely pink) and white sapwood tinged with fine brown lines. Overall warm tan-brown with enough pinkish-red warmth that you'd never mistake it for plain oak or alder. Mineral streaking, small closed knots, and pin knots acceptable. Parts may be all heartwood or all sapwood.

pecan-hickory-rustic-knotty.name: Pecan-Hickory Rustic Knotty
pecan-hickory-rustic-knotty.description: Allows for mineral streaking, larger open knots, knots with star cracks, clusters of pin knots. Stark variations between warm tan-brown heartwood with a clear pinkish-red undertone (obvious but secondary to the tan-brown base — not purely tan, not purely pink) and white sapwood tinged with fine brown lines. Overall warm tan-brown with enough pinkish-red warmth that you'd never mistake it for plain oak or alder. Parts may be all heartwood or all sapwood.

sapele-ribbon-grain-mahogany.name: Sapele Ribbon Grain Mahogany
sapele-ribbon-grain-mahogany.description: Quarter Sawn Sapele with ribbon striped appearance. Warm golden-brown base with alternating lighter and slightly darker bands creating the characteristic ribbon effect. Medium saturation — NOT dark or chocolate brown. Match the swatch closely: the overall tone should be a warm, luminous golden-copper brown, not deep or heavy.

ash-select.name: Ash Select
ash-select.description: Pale cream with straw-yellow undertones. Mild, subtle cathedral grain patterns. Featuring a moderately open grain pattern, Ash Select displays colors ranging from cream to a very light brown heartwood with lighter colored sapwood. Though some color variation and mineral may be allowed on the face. Reduced allowance for small closed knots and pin knots.

fir-vertical-grain.name: Fir Vertical Grain
fir-vertical-grain.description: Fir Vertical Grain is most known for its distinct, straight grain pattern and warm tones. The most commonly used species is Douglas Fir. Light peachy-tan base color with tight, evenly spaced vertical grain lines in a slightly darker warm tone. The material is known for its softness and is not advised for heavy traffic and use areas.
